deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—i.e., it never articulates that the Socratic approach supports active reasoning, retention, or skill development. In Transcript 1 the tutor does say 'I'm not going to fill it for you' (Turn 8) but offers no rationale for why that constraint benefits the student's learning.

In Transcripts 2 and 3, students make clear progress (e.g., Transcript 2 Turn 10: student articulates the boundary-checking method correctly; Transcript 3 Turn 7: student makes a decisive, well-reasoned choice). The tutor moves forward without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly asks for the same re-explanation of the assignment and the tutor cycles through multiple reframings (buckets, workflow, structure) without consolidating or advancing. By Turn 7 the student is still asking for the same thing and the tutor has not switched to a meaningfully different strategy or suggested moving on from the meta-confusion to just attempting a row.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well overall: it avoids giving submission-ready answers, keeps key idea generation student-led, scaffolds appropriately for cooperative students, and maintains a supportive tone throughout. The main weaknesses are: (1) no explicit rationale for the tutoring approach is ever offered to the student; (2) no reflection prompts after student breakthroughs; and (3) in Transcript 1, the tutor cycles through multiple reframings of the same meta-question without consolidating or decisively redirecting the student, triggering the redundancy/spiraling deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is clean—the tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks the student to generate ideas before confirming them. 1.2 is clean—the tutor builds on student progress and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained (1.3.A.a), and after clear student improvements in Transcripts 2 and 3 the tutor never prompts reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points for Transcript 1, where the student asks for the same re-explanation across seven turns and the tutor keeps reframing without consolidating or decisively shifting strategy. 2.2 is clean—no assignment misunderstanding goes uncorrected and no off-topic drift occurs. 3.1 and 3.2 are clean—responses are appropriately concise and the tone is consistently supportive and formative.
